problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem A (continuous) — the Ebola problem. Teams had to model the spread of the 2014 West Africa Ebola epidemic, then build a realistic system for delivering a newly-announced cure: where medicine/vaccine should be manufactured and delivered, the speed of manufacturing, the total quantity needed, and the delivery (logistics) system. It is fundamentally a coupled epidemiology + operations-research / logistics problem. This Outstanding paper (Team 34317) tackles all sub-questions in an end-to-end chain: (1) predict cases/deaths via a stage-structured compartmental ODE model for Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone; (2) site delivery hubs; (3) route trucks; (4) compute medicine quantity and manufacturing ramp-up speed; closing with sensitivity analysis and a results letter to the World Medical Association.
models_used: SEIIRF stage-structured compartmental ODE model (S, E, I1, I2, R, F = susceptible / exposed / first-stage infected / serious second-stage infected / recovered / dead-awaiting-burial) — a SEIR variant extended with funeral (F) transmission and split infection stages, adapted from Eisenberg et al. (Lancet ID 2015, ref [5])

Advanced/extended SEIIRF model adding a hospital compartment (H1, H2) and 7 extra parameters (beta_H, h1, h2, gamma3, gamma4, delta_1H, delta_2H), encoding 17.6% hospital-acquired infection observed in Sierra Leone — 16 parameters total per country
Cumulative case / death observables via time integrals: y_C = integral of alpha*E(t) dt (total cases), y_D = integral of delta2*gamma2*I2(t) dt (deaths)

Genetic Algorithm coupled with Least-Squares for parameter estimation (fitness = sum of absolute errors of cumulative cases + cumulative deaths vs WHO data), replacing the reference paper's plain least-squares whose parameters had gone stale

Hierarchical agglomerative clustering with single-linkage (nearest-neighbor recurrence formula on a reticular/road-network distance matrix D) to group prefecture capitals into 'city groups' / delivery regions
P-Center facility-location model (minimize max/total distance Z = sum D_ij*y_ij) for the fairness principle of siting delivery hubs
P-Median facility-location model (minimize Y = sum w_i*D_ij*y_ij, weighted by infected count) for the efficiency principle

Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) as a 0-1 mixed-integer program with Miller-Tucker-Zemlin subtour-elimination constraints (u_i - u_j + n*x_ij <= n-1), solved in LINGO, to build circular truck routes from each hub
Urgency index prob(i) = H1 in a place / H1 across all three countries — a proportional allocation rule for distributing medicine to locations

Linear manufacturing ramp-up model V1 = k1*t + b1 (medicine) and V2 = k2*t + b2 (vaccine), capped at H1 demand; total quantity via w = integral V1(t) dt ~ 3400 units; medicine/vaccine fed back as sink/source terms into the SEIIRF ODEs
Maximal-coverage location model (extension): maximize A = sum w_i*c_ij*y_ij with a time-decay coverage degree c_ij
key_techniques: Iterative model refinement narrative: started from a published baseline model, showed it failed on Jan 2015 data ('Results are Not So Reasonable'), then re-estimated parameters, then added hospital compartments ('Results are Reasonable') — making the modeling process itself the story
Re-fitting stale literature parameters with GA+LSQ on fresh WHO data (Oct–Dec 2014) and validating out-of-sample on held-out Jan 1 – Feb 3, 2015 data — genuine train/test discipline
Grounding a structural modeling choice in data: a pie chart showing 17.6% of Sierra Leone infections were hospital-acquired justified adding the hospital compartment rather than asserting it

Chaining models so outputs feed inputs: SEIIRF's H1 (first-stage hospital patients) drives both the urgency-index allocation weights and the manufacturing-demand cap, and the manufactured medicine/vaccine are injected back as terms in the ODEs — a closed feedback loop across the whole paper

Coupling a clustering→facility-location→routing pipeline behind a single 'general algorithm' flowchart with a feasibility gate (can a car finish every route within one day? if not, increase number of hubs and re-cluster)

Encoding humanitarian values as explicit competing objective principles — fairness (P-Center) vs efficiency (P-Median) — and explicitly weighting fairness higher because one-day delivery made time differences negligible
Operationalizing 'locations' and 'quantity' by writing out the team's own definitions of the problem's ambiguous words before modeling (section 1.2)
Sensitivity analysis by perturbing key parameters (beta1, alpha, gamma1) by +/-10% and the manufacturing initial speed b1, showing outputs stay stable

Concrete, decision-ready deliverables: named delivery cities (Dubreka, Kouroussa, Macenta in Guinea; Grand Bassa in Liberia; Tonkolili in Sierra Leone), explicit named circular routes, route distances vs the 80 km/h one-day feasibility threshold, and a single headline number (~3400 units of medicine)
why_it_won: "From a judge's lens it wins on completeness, realism, and an honest, data-driven modeling arc. It answers every sub-question of the problem and chains them coherently — most teams nail epidemiology OR logistics, not both stitched into one feedback loop. The standout move is intellectual honesty made into structure: it adopts a published model, demonstrates with held-out data that it fails on recent data, fixes the parameters with GA+LSQ, then upgrades the structure (hospital compartment) and shows the improved fit — section titles literally read 'Results are Not So Reasonable' then 'Results are Reasonable.' Judges reward visible validation and out-of-sample testing, which this paper does explicitly. Its modeling choices are justified by evidence (the 17.6% hospital-infection pie chart) rather than assertion. It encodes the humanitarian heart of the problem as a fairness-vs-efficiency objective trade-off (P-Center vs P-Median), showing modeling maturity. And it produces concrete, decision-ready outputs a real WMA could act on: named hub cities, explicit truck routes checked against a one-day feasibility constraint, a manufacturing ramp schedule, and a single quantity (~3400 units). The framing (Ban Ki-moon epigraph, 'A Battle Without Gun and Smoke', closing letter) gives it a memorable, communicative identity that judges remember."
section_highlights: "Summary (p.1) front-loads all three results with hard numbers per country (e.g. Guinea ~4000 cases / 2500 deaths by Apr 1 2015), so a judge sees the payload immediately. Section 1.2 'Our understanding of the words' disambiguates the loose problem terms (locations, quantity, speed) before any math. Section IV is the spine: 4.4 'Results are Not So Reasonable' → 4.5 GA+LSQ re-fit → 4.6 'Advanced Model' (hospital compartment, justified by the 4.4 pie chart) → 4.7 'Results are Reasonable' with out-of-sample validation. Section V cleanly separates the two siting objectives into 5.5 fairness (P-Center) and 5.6 efficiency (P-Median). Section VII gives a reproducible 'general algorithm' flowchart with a one-day-route feasibility gate and walks all three countries with named cities, routes, and distances. Section VIII closes the loop, feeding manufacturing output back into the ODEs and integrating to ~3400 units. Section XI is a real sensitivity analysis (+/-10% on beta1, alpha, gamma1, and on b1). The closing letter to the WMA translates everything into plain executive language."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The digitized chart data in this markdown is clearly OCR-mangled (many figures show implausible perfectly-linear placeholder series and garbled map city names like 'BORGAL' repeated), so the exact fitted curves cannot be trusted from this file — but the methodology is intact and readable

Incomplete execution under time pressure, admitted by the team: future-case prediction was only carried out for Guinea, not Liberia or Sierra Leone ('we must say sorry to people who live in Liberia and Sierra Leone'); the no-boundary cross-country scheme and the maximal-coverage extension were defined but never solved

Parameter proliferation risk: the advanced model carries 16 parameters per country fit by GA on limited WHO daily data — high overfitting risk and weak identifiability; the sensitivity analysis only perturbs 3 of them, so robustness is under-demonstrated

Strong simplifying assumptions: no population flow between regions, demand concentrated only at prefecture capitals (ignoring rural/lower-administrative spread), construction/storage costs ignored, and vaccine effect dropped entirely because it was 'small vs susceptible' — convenient but unjustified quantitatively
The linear manufacturing ramp (V = kt+b) is acknowledged by the team as 'maybe too ideal'; real vaccine scale-up is non-linear/capacity-constrained
The fairness/efficiency weighting is qualitative ('we endow more weight to fairness') with no quantitative weight reported, so the actual siting decision is not fully reproducible
Some notation is inconsistent / typo-ridden (e.g. delta_1*delta_2 vs delta_1, the coverage piecewise c_ij has two branches both labeled t>=t_m), reflecting contest-time haste
reusable_patterns: The 'baseline → diagnose failure on held-out data → re-fit → enrich structure → re-validate' loop is a transferable template for any modeling problem: never present only the final model; show the journey and out-of-sample validation, and name sections after the result quality.
When extending a compartmental/ODE model, justify each new compartment with a data artifact (a pie chart, a proportion) rather than prose — turns an assumption into evidence.

Use GA + least-squares (or any global optimizer + loss) to re-estimate parameters from a literature model on fresh data, with a fitness that sums errors across multiple observables (cases AND deaths).

Encode competing stakeholder values as two named optimization objectives and pick/weight between them explicitly — here fairness=P-Center (minimax distance) vs efficiency=P-Median (demand-weighted) — instead of a single vague objective.

Pipeline location problems as cluster → site (P-center/P-median) → route (TSP/MILP), wrapped in one 'general algorithm' flowchart with an explicit real-world feasibility gate (e.g. one-day route at 80 km/h) that loops back and adds hubs when violated.

Close the loop between sub-models: let the epidemic model's compartment (H1) define both the logistics allocation weights and the manufacturing demand cap, and feed the manufactured supply back as source/sink terms in the ODEs — demonstrates an integrated system, not disconnected parts.
Solve TSP as a 0-1 MILP with Miller-Tucker-Zemlin subtour-elimination constraints in LINGO — a clean, citable, reproducible formulation for contest routing.
Produce decision-ready outputs: name the actual cities/routes/quantities and check them against a hard constraint, then mirror them in a one-page executive letter to the problem's stated client.
Front-load the summary with per-scenario headline numbers, and pre-define ambiguous problem words in their own section before modeling.
